witness.expan: Tamiḻneṟi Viḷakkam. Edited by U. Vē. Cāminātaiyar. 1937 1st ed.
Tamiḻneṟi Viḷakkam. Edited by U. Vē. Cāminātaiyar. 1937 1st ed. [ante correctionem]
Tamiḻneṟi Viḷakkam. Edited by U. Vē. Cāminātaiyar. 1937 1st ed. [post correctionem]
Tamiḻneṟi Viḷakkam. Edited by U. Vē. Cāminātaiyar. 1947 2nd ed.
Tamiḻneṟi Viḷakkam. Edited by U. Vē. Cāminātaiyar. 1947 2nd ed. [ante correctionem]
Tamiḻneṟi Viḷakkam. Edited by U. Vē. Cāminātaiyar. 1947 2nd ed. [post correctionem]
A Critical Edition and Study of Akapporul Vilakkam [ante correctionem]
A Critical Edition and Study of Akapporul Vilakkam [post correctionem]
Kuṟuntokai 88. Maturaik Katakkaṇṇaṉ: the confidante / SHE
Kuṟuntokai 130. Veḷḷi Vīti(yār): the confidante / SHE

lg.l: viṉātalum viṭuttaluṅ kaḻaṟalu maṟuttalu
nōtalum yāṇṭeṉa viṉṉuḻi yeṉṟalu
mātaraik kāṇṭalu maṉṉiya vakuttaluṅ
kiḻavōṟ kicaittalu māṟṟāṉ kiḷattalum
paḻavarai viṭuttalum pāṅkaṟ kūṭṭam
[The hero's friend] questioning [the hero], [the hero] letting out [the secret], [the hero's friend] reproaching [the hero], [the hero] refuting [that reproach],
[the hero's friend’s] suffering, [when asked by his friend], “Where?”, [the hero] saying, “In such a place”,
[the hero's friend] seeing the woman, [the hero's friend] classifying [her as] agreeable,
[the hero's friend] speaking to the hero, [the hero] making it clear that he cannot bear [joining the heroine],
and sending [her] off to the old onesHer elders here are her family. — these are 'the union [by means of] the hero's friend'.

div.p: e-tu. pāṅkaṟ kūṭṭam ām āṟuṇarttutaṉutaliṟṟu.
The quoted [cūttiram] intends to make known the way in which 'the union [by means of] the hero's friend occurs'.

pāṅkaṉ ṟalaimakaṟkuṟṟa viṉātalun talaimakaṉ iṉṉateṉṟalum pāṅkaṉ kaḻaṟalun talaimakaṉ kaḻaṟṟetir maṟuttalum pāṅkaṉōtalum evviṭattevvuru veṉṟalun talaimakaṉ iṉṉa viṭattiṉṉa vuru veṉṟalum pāṅkaṉ avviṭattavvuruk kāṇṭalun talaimakaṉaip perumai kūṟalum peyarntu vantu talaimakaṟkuraittalun talaimakaṉ ṟalaimakaḷaik kaṇṭaṇaitalum uṟaviṉ āṟṟāṉ kūṟalum viṭuttalum pāṅkaṟ kūṭṭam ām eṉṟa vāṟu.

The hero's friend questioning the hero about what has happened [to him], the hero saying, “It is so”, the hero's friend reproaching [the hero], [the hero] refuting [his friend's] reproach, the hero's friend's suffering, [the hero's friend] saying, “In what place and in what form?”, [the hero] saying, “In such a place and in such a form",What seems to be one situation in the cūttiram — yāṇṭeṉa viṉṉuḻi yeṉṟal ('[when asked] “Where?” saying, “In such a place.”'), I take to be treated as two here. The Tamil allows one to understand this as one, but as this commentary closely follows the IAC, I have chosen to mirror its construction and take these as distinct situations. the hero's friend seeing that form in that place, [the hero's friend] speaking of [the hero's] greatness, [the hero's friend] leaving and telling [the hero about the place], the hero seeing the heroine and approaching [her], [the hero] not being able to bear the experience and speaking, and sending [the heroine] off — [these] are 'the union [by means of] the hero's friend'.

lg.l: muṉṉiṉai muṭippatoṉ ṟuḷatukoṉ mūvakai
nuṇṇiya paṉuvali ṉuḻaintuko
leṉṉaikol vāṭiya taṇṇaṉiṉ ṉeḻilē

Is there something [you] considered previously that was accomplished?I think the point here is the contrast between and active planned action, which is suggested in the question here, or an unplanned occurrence as suggested by the next question.

Has something entered [your mind]nuḻaital can mean 'to squeeze in through a tight space'. This is similar to the idea of something 'worming its way in', implying that the process and result is not wanted.

because ofpaṉuvaliṉ can also be understood as a comparative, in which case this would read: “Has something entered [your] mind, like / such as the fine compositions...” the fine compositions of three types?The three types of composition the poet is referring to are icai ('music'), iyal ('treatises') and nāṭakam ('drama').

What is it? Your beauty is fading, O lord.I do not have a suitable or particularly satisfactory translation for aṇṇal, one of the most common designations for the hero. Something like 'majesty' might come closest to the actual meaning of the word, but would sound too pompous if used on the translation. I use 'lord', as it implies a sense of superiority, and sounds less ridiculous, but I accept that it flattens the nuance of the Tamil somewhat.

div.p: eṉpatu viṉātal.
This is [the hero's friend] questioning [the hero].

lg.l: ciṟuveḷ ḷaravi ṉavvarik kuruḷai
kāṉa yāṉai yaṇaṅki yāaṅ
kiḷaiyaṇ muḷaivā ḷeyiṟṟaḷ
vaḷaiyuṭaik kaiyaḷem maṇaṅki yōḷē
Like the young one with pretty stripes
of the small white snake torments a forest elephant,
the young girl with sprouting bright teeth
and bangles on her arms torments us.

div.p: eṉpatu pāṅkaṟkut talaimakaṉ kūṟiyatu.
This is the hero having told his friend.

lg.l: poruntā tamma puṉaiyiḻai yarivai
muruntēr muṟuva ṉōkki
varuntiṉa ṉeṉpatu peruntakai peritē
O, it is not appropriate what was said by the most worthy man,

that, after looking at the sprout-like smileIn light of the next poem, where the hero is attracted by her innocent and/or foolish glance, one might question if nōkki can be understood as 'a glance'. murunt' ēr muṟuval would then qualify the glance. The sprout-like teeth she shows when she smiles represent her youth and innocence, conveying something similar to maṭam in the next poem.
of the young woman with ornate jewels, he suffered greatly.

div.p: eṉpatu pāṅkaṉ kaḻaṟiyatu.
This is the hero's friend having scolded [the hero].

lg.l: kāṇkuvi rāyiṟ kaḻaṟalir maṉṉō
pūṇpuṉai vaḷariḷa vaṉamulai
māṇkuḻai mātar maṭaṅkeḻu nōkkē
If you were to see it, surely you would not scold me!
The innocent glance of the young woman with brilliant earrings
and growing young beautiful breasts decorated with ornaments.

div.p: eṉpatu kaḻaṟṟetir maṟuttatu.
This is [the hero] having refuted the scolding.

lg.l: pulantuṟai pōkiya neñcuniṟai yaḻintu
kalaṅkuva tāyiṉ mātō
cilampaṉai yārō teḷikkunar piṟarē

If indeed his heart, which continues suffering,One can split this differently — pulam tuṟai. In this case, it would mean 'the heart that goes from the way/place of wisdom/the senses'. As we find the phrase, pulantu uṟai in KT334 and PN210, that is how it is split here.
loses its self-control and becomes distressed,

who elseIn the sense that the situation is hopeless. The hero is meant to be a great man and yet he refuses to see sense and the correct course of action. This should have been the response to the scolding he received from his friend. will pacifyThe primary meaning of teḷittal is 'to make clear'. In its usage here, it 'makes clear' the heart of the hero, removing its distress. the man from the mountain-slope.

div.p: eṉpatu pāṅkaṉ piḻaittaṉaṉ eṉṟāṟṟāṉ āyiṟṟu.

This is the hero's friend having become one who is able to say, “I am wrong."This could also be a statement acknowledging that the hero is acting incorrectly, and thus doing wrong, or that there is something wrong with him and that he is state of confusion: "He is wrong". The forms are homophonous.

lg.l: vāḻvati yāvatu kollō vāṉpukaḻc
cūḻkaḻa laṇṇa ṉeñca
māḻtuya reyta vaṇaṅkiya vaṇaṅkē
How will the heart of the lord, praised by the heavens,

with warrior anklets around [his feet],I have currently taken vāṉ pukaḻ as phrase qualifying the hero, where pukaḻ is a verbal root functioning as a peyareccam. It is also possible to construe it with the following phrase - cūḻ kaḻal aṇṇal, in which case the anklets would be enveloped by sky-high fame, and pukaḻ would be a root noun. survive the torment

which plagued itThe figura etymologica aṇaṅkiya aṇaṅkē can either be understood literally as in this translation, or as 'terrible torment'. So: “How will the heart survive the terrible torment...” as it fellLiterally: “so that it attained a deep sorrow”, but as it is idiomatic to talk of 'one falling into a state of x' in English and since the sorrow is deep, this is a justifiable departure from the literal translation. into a deep sorrow.

div.p: eṉpatu pāṅkaṉ iṭam viṉāyiṟṟu.
This is the hero's friend having asked about the place [where the lovers met].

lg.l: kaṇṇē kāviyaṅ kayamalar nutalē
taṇṇiḷa matiyeṉat takumē patiyum
perumalaic cāraṟ cīṟū
rarumai vāṅkiya vāyiḻai taṉakkē
[Her] eyes are the pretty water-tank blossoms of the lotus, and [her] forehead
is fit to be called the cool young moon, and [her] abode:
on the slopes of the great mountain is the small town

of the one with beautiful jewels who dragged me into difficulty.This peyareccam phrase - arumai vāṅkiya - could also have the agent and object reversed: “whom I dragged from difficulty...”. In that case, this could be an allusion to the hero lifting the heroine out of the rising floods — a common trope found in this style of poetry, also found later in this collection (see TNV 22, poem 1). Another alternative would be to understand arumai differently, as 'preciousness' or 'greatness'. In that case, the heroine would be 'dragging away' the hero's 'greatness', i.e. causing him pain. arumai, however, is not a quality I commonly associate with the hero's state. Either way, that is the idea this phrase most likely conveys. This follows the PK verses cited where the hero is said to 'wither' on account of the heroine. One last option, which I think highly unlikely but I mention to be complete, is that this might be referring to the heroine, "who had taken [on a state of] preciousness". This, however, would divert significantly from the source material.

div.p: eṉpatu talaimakaṉ kuṟiyuraittatu.
This is the hero having told [his friend] the location of the tryst.

lg.l: vēṅkai yoḷvī kāntaḷoṭu kamaḻum
pūntaṇ cāralu mituvē yēntilai
yayilvē laṇṇal kūṟiya
mayilēr cāyal vaṇṇamu mituvē
This must beThis is the translation for the emphatic ituvē. the flowering cool slope,
fragrant with kino trees and glory-lilies with bright mature blossoms,

and this must be the form with the grace of a peacockvaṇṇam is polysemous. Here it could reasonably also mean, 'beauty' or 'nature'. It is translated with 'form' following the IAC passage quoted before: ivviṭamē avaṉāṟ kāṇappaṭṭa iṭamum, ivvuruvē avaṉāṟ kāṇappaṭṭa uruvum, where there is clear mention of the heroine's 'form'.
the lord with a sharp spear with an excellent leaf-tipēntu-tal can also mean 'to raise'. This image has clear sexual connotations, which would be further emphasised with such a translation. spoke of.

div.p: eṉpatu pāṅkaṉ etirp paṭṭatu.
This is the hero's friend having encountered [the heroine].

lg.l: nōtaka vuṭaittē neñca matimicai
mātark kuvaḷai malarmalarn taṉṉa
karuntaṭaṅ kaṇkaṇ ṭāṟṟiyap
peruntakai yaṇṇalaik kaḻaṟiṉaṉ peritē

[My] heart deserves to suffer!This could also understood as: “His heart deserves to suffer!”. In that case the focus of the poem and the companion's lament changes slightly. Instead of a poem about primarily about self-censure, this would read as one about the mental fortitude of the hero.
I harshly scolded the most worthy lord,
who saw her dark wide eyes like the beautiful blooming
blue waterlily blossom upon which lay the moon
and was able to bear it.

div.p: eṉpatu talaimakaṉ āṟṟal kūṟiyatu.
This is [the hero's friend] having talked about the hero's ability to bear [her beauty].

lg.l: āytaḷir potuḷiya vītatai taṇciṉaik
kāykatir nuḻaiyāk kaṭipoḻil
yāvayi ṉōrum viḻaivuṟun takaittē
The sheltered grove full of choice buds and
with cooling branches with crowded blossoms
which the scorching rays cannot enter through
can makeLiterally: “the grove is suitable for anyone to feel desire” or “it is suitable for anyone to feel desire in the grove...”. anyone, whoever they may be, feel desire.

div.p: eṉpatu pāṅkaṉ peyarntu vantu talaimakaṟkuraittatu.
This is the hero's friend having told the hero [about it], after leaving [the trysting-spot] and coming back.

lg.l: taṉimai neñcattu muṉivukaṇ ṇakaṟṟaliṉ
viṉaimāṇ pāvai yaṉṉa
puṉaiyiḻai mātarum pōṉṟatip poḻilē
Since it drives away affliction from the lonely heart,

this grove also resembles the young woman,In mātarum, it is not clear what the function of the particle -um is. There is clearly no co-ordination. Perhaps a nuance of remaining or being left over? This is how it is translated, though it is unclear what meaning it conveys. adorned with jewels,

who is like a gloriously crafted image.It is not clear to what the pāvai refers. It is a polysemic word that could reasonably mean either 'a doll/figurine/statue' or 'a painting/image' in this occurrence. This exact noun-phrase occurs twice in the Caṅkam corpus (see NA185.11, Neṭunalvāṭai 101). In NA185, it is a reference to a figure, supposedly carved by the gods in the Kolli hills that enchants those who lay their eyes on it. In the Neṭunalvāṭai , it seems to refer to small female-shaped lamps made by the Greeks. Whichever way, the implication in both examples is that the pāvai is an enchanting female figure, which is presumably the idea here.

div.p: eṉpatu talaimakaṉ avviṭattuc ceṉṟatu.
This is the hero having gone to that place.

lg.l: teyva māka vaiyuṟu neñcam
poyyā tāyiṉiṉ cevvāy payanta
kiḷipurai kiḷaviyām peṟuka
voḷiyiḻai maṭantai yuyirpeyarp paritē
If the heart that possesses the power to discernvaiyuṟu here refers to a fineness or subtlety of intellect, i.e. the power of correct discernment. [if you are a] god does not lie,
let us obtain the parakeet-like speech,In other words, he needs to hear her speak to know that she is not a deity. born from [your] sweet red mouth,
O young woman with the bright jewels!

Life departing is a difficult thing.I find this slightly ambiguous. In the PK, the difficult thing will be the return of the hero's life after it has departed, which is the natural result of not hearing her speak and living in doubt. Here the same may be implied by the nominal derivation peyarppu ('departing').

div.p: eṉpatu talaimakaṉ aṇaital uṟaviṉ āṟṟāṉ kūṟiyatu.

This is the hero having declared his inability to bear the experience of approaching [the heroine].Alternatively: “The hero talks about being disconsolate on account of the experience of approaching...”

lg.l: kāviyaṅ karuṅkaṭ cevvāyp paintoṭi
pūviri curimeṉ kūntalum
vēypurai tōḷu maṇaṅkumā lemmē
Pretty dark eyes like blue waterlilies, a red mouth, gold bangles,
curled delicate tresses where flowers spread,This could either refer to the flowers being spread around her hair or that they have blossomed and are open.
and bamboo-like shoulders torment us indeed.

div.p: ituvum atu.
And this is that.

lg.l: perumalarp pirinta tirumaka ḷammala
raruṅkavi ṉeytac ceṉṟu cērntāṅ
karuṅkalat toṉmaṉai poliyap
peruntakait tōḷi peyarntaṉai ceṉmē
As Śri, who had separated from the great blossom,
went and rejoined it so that the flower obtained rare beauty,
so that the ancient house of rare ornaments flourishes,
O you with the most worthy shoulders, go and returnThis translates the muṟṟeccam construction, peyarntaṉai ceṉmē!

div.p: eṉpatu talaimakaḷai viṭuttal. 16.
This is [the hero] sending off the heroine.

standOff.note: Cf. TP499n.2: paṅkoṭu taḻāl
Cf. TP102n.9: niṟpavai niṉaii nikaḻpavai yuraippiṉuṅ | kūṟṟaṅ kāṭṭiya vāyil peṭpiṉum |
Cf. IA3.1-2: āṅṅaṉam puṉarnta kiḻavōṉ ṟaṉvayiṟ | pāṅka ṉōriṟ kuṟitalaip peyartalum |
Iḷampūraṉar and Nacciṉārkkiṉiyar both understand that these lines in TP102n refer to the hero's friend's reproach of the hero. This is the only mention in the TP concerning this situation.

The IA parallel simply mentions the existence of a meeting in which the hero's friend plays some sort of role, but there is no elaboration on the figure. The first instance of any expansion of the situation comes in Nakkīraṉār's commentary. As before, it is quite certain that it was the source for this cūttiram, but as the commentary shows closer similarities to the wording found in the IAC, it is provided there.

AV136 gives a list of situations that one could try and map onto that provided here, but the similarities are not great. Where one finds strong similarities are in AV137, the exposition on the situations listed in AV136. These are parallels to the TNVC and both seem to follow the IAC closely in content and phrasing.

One expects the masculine singular here rather than the honorific/plural, which is reported in C1. The standard designation for the hero in poetics, whatever the terminology used, is found in the masculine sigular. See AV137.4 (provided below as a parallel for the commentary) for an example of kiḻavōṉ. This situation is glossed in the commentary by talaimakaṟku uraittal, a masculine singular dative form.
Cf. IAC3, p. 62: eṉṉaikollō emperumāṟku iṟṟai vēṟupāṭṭu vāṭṭameṉṉum.
Cf. IAC3, p. 63: vakai kaṭaippiṭityatu viṉāyiṉa pāṅkaṟku nerunal ivvakaiyār oruvaraik kaṇṭēṟku eṉṉuḷḷam puḷḷattuvaḻi veḷḷampōla ōṭi ivvakaittāyiṟṟeṉṟu collum.
Cf. IAC3, p. 64: ivvāṟu collakkēṭṭa pāṅkaṉ kaḻaṟum.
Cf. IAC3, p. 65: ivvāṟu pāṅkaṉkaḻaṟat talaimakaṉ āṟṟāṉāyc colluñ coṟkaḷ kaḻaṟṟetirmaṟaiyeṉpatu.
Cf. IAC3, p. 66: ivvāṟu talaimakaṉ taṉatāṟṟāmaiyiṟ kaḻaṟṟetir maṟutturaippak kēṭṭa pāṅkaṉ... eṉak kavaṉṟāṉ.
Cf. IAC3, p. 67: evviṭattu ettaṉmaittu niṉṟār kāṇapaṭṭa uruveṉṉum.
Cf. IAC3, p. 67: ivviṭattu ittaṉmaittu eṉṟār kāṇapaṭṭa uruveṉṉum.
Cf. IAC3, p. 69: kaṇṭu ivviṭamē avaṉāṟ kāṇappaṭṭa iṭamum, ivvuruvē avaṉāṟ kāṇappaṭṭa uruvumeṉat tuṇiyum.
Cf. IAC3, p. 70: iṟantupaṭātu eṉkāṟum vanta iruntuṇaip peruntakkāṉai yāṉ periyatōriṭaṉaṉṟik kaḻaṟiṉēṉeṉa niṉaikkum.
Cf. IAC3, p. 69: avvakai niṉaittut talaimakaṉaic ceṉṟeyti yāṉkaṇṭa iṭam mētakkatē kāṇ... paṉpiṟṟeṉṉum.
Cf. IAC3, p. 73: aṭaiya, maṟaivatu kāṇātu mātupaṭunōkki taṉ maṇikkāntaṇ melviralāṟ pōtupuraineṭuṅkaṇ putaittāḷ.
Cf. IAC3, p. 72: āṟṟāmaiyāvatu piṟitevvuṇarvumiṉṟi avvaṟṟāmai tāṉēyāvatu. avvāṟṟāmai āṟṟuvatoṉṟaṉaip paṟṟum. āṟṟuvatu piṟitiṉmaiyiṉ orucoṟ collum.
Cf. AV137.1-2: pāṅkaṉ | ṟalaivaṉai yuṟṟatu viṉātalum
Cf. AV137.2-3: talaivaṉ | yuṟṟa tuṟaittalum
Cf. AV137.3-4: pāṅkaṉ | kaḻaṟalum
Cf. AV137.4-5: kiḻavōṉ kaḻaṟṟetir maṟuttalum |
Cf. AV137.6-7: pāṅkaṉ | taṉmaṉat taḻuṅkalum
Cf. AV137.8: evviṭat tevviyaṟ ṟeṉṟalum
Cf. AV137.8-9: avaṉaḵ | tivviṭat tivviyaṟ ṟeṉṟalum
Cf. AV137.11: iṟaiviyaik kāṇṭalum
Cf. AV137.12: talaiviyai viyattalum
Cf. AV137.14: talaivaṉ cēṟalum talaiviyai kāṇṭalum
Cf. AV137.15-16: talaiviyaip | pāṅkiyoṭu varukeṉap pakartalum

The parallels here are sometimes not exact. The IAC introduces situations by explaining them and doesn't normally provide a specific term for them, whereas the TNV commentary does. In some of cases, the TNV commentator uses the wording from the IAC. The most pertinent part of the corresponding passage is provided.
This final situation is not found in the IAC, but only in the AV.
Cf. PK23.2-4 (IAC3, p.63): koḻuntamiḻi | ṉoṇtuṟai mēluḷḷa mōṭiya tōvaṉṟi yuṟṟatuṇṭō | taṇtuṟai vācintai vāṭiyeṉ ṉāṅkol taḷarkiṉṟatē ||
Cf. TK20.2-4: uyarmatiṟ kūṭali ṉāyntavōṇ ṭīntamiḻiṉ | tuṟaivāy nuḻainta ṉaiyōvaṉṟi yēḻicaic cūḻalpukkō | iṟaivā taṭavarait tōṭkaṉko lāmpukun teytiyatē ||
Cf. KT129.5-6 (IAC3, p.64): katuppayal viḷaṅkuñ ciṟunutal | putukkōḷ yāṉaiyiṟ piṇittaṟṟā lemmē ||

Cf. PK25 (IAC3, p.53): aḷaiyā raraviṉ kuruḷai yaṇaṅka vaṟivaḻintu | tuḷaiyār neṭuṅkaik kaḷiṟu neṭuṅkit tuyarvatupōl | vaḷaiyār muḷaiyeyiṟ ṟarmaṉṉaṉ māṟaṉvaṇ kūṭalaṉṉa | viḷaiyā roruva raṇaṅkanain tālyā niṉaikiṉṟatē ||

Cf. TK21: kōmpik kotuṅkimē yāmaññai kuñcaraṅ kōḷiḻaikkum | pāmpaip piṭittup paṭaṇkiḻit tāṅkap paṇaimulaikkē | tēmpaṟ ṟuṭiyiṭai māṉmaṭa nōkkitil laiccivaṉṟāḷ | āmpoṟ ṟaṭamalar cūṭumeṉ ṉāṟṟa lakaṟṟiyatē ||

This poem is also quoted in the commentary to IA3 to exemplify the situation where the hero reveals to the hero's friend that he is pining after the heroine. Other poems that employ the same image of something (which represents the heroine) entrancing an elephant (the hero) are provided as parallels.
Cf. IAC3, p.64: appeṟṟiyāya nī iṉṉatōriṭattu iṉṉatōruruvukaṇṭu eṉṉuṇarvaḻiyap pōnteṉeṉṟal takkiṉreṉṉum.
Though there are poems quoted in IAC3 for this situation, the TNV poem more closely resemebles the prose summary of these poems.
Cf. AN130 (IAC3, p.65-66): amma vāḻi kōḷir muṉṉiṉṟu | kaṇṭaṉi rāyiṟ kaḻaṟaliṟ maṉṉō | ...uṇkaṇ | mātar vāṇmuka mataiiya nōkkē ||
Cf. PK30.3-4 (IAC3, p.65): vaṇṭār kuḻaṉmaṭa maṅkai matarvaimeṉ ṉōkkameṉpōṟ | kaṇṭā ruḷarē luraiyār piṟaviṉṉa kaṭṭuraiyē ||
Cf. PK31.3-4 (IAC3, p.65): paṇkoṇṭa collam maṭantai mukattuppaim pūṅkuvaḷaik | kaṇkaṇṭa piṉṉai yuraiyī ruraittalik kaṭṭuraiyē ||
Cf. TK23.4: kāṇiṟ kaḻaṟilai kaṇṭilai meṉṟōṭ karumpiṉaiyē ||
Just the main sentence of AN130 is quoted. It is a long poem and this is the relevant section.
Cf. PK33.3-4 (IAC3, p.67): niṟaiyu maṟivuṅ kalaṅki nilaitaḷaru | meṉṟāṟ ṟeruṭṭaval lāriṉi yāriv virunilattē ||

Cf. PK36 (IAC3, p.67-68): aṭivaṇṇan tāmarai yāṭara valku larattamaṅkai | koṭivaṇṇa nuṇṇiṭai kovvaicev vāykoṅkaikōṅ karumpiṉ | paṭivaṇṇañ ceṅkōṟ parāṅkucaṉ kollippaim pūmpoḻilvāy | vaṭivaṇṇa vēṟkaṇṇi ṉāleṉṉai vāṭṭiya vāṇutaṟkē ||

Cf. PK37 (IAC3, p.68): tirumā mukantiṅkaḷ ceṅkaya luṇkaṇcem poṟcuṇaṅkēr | vārumā mulaimaṇic ceppiṇai vāṉavaṉ kāṉamuṉṉak | kurumā neṭumatiṟ kōṭṭāṟ ṟaraṅkoṇṭa teṉṉaṉkaṉṉip | perumāṉ varōtayaṉ kolliyañ cāralp peṇkoṭikkē ||

Cf. PK38 (IAC3, p.68): kaṭittaṭam viṇṭa kamala mukaṅkama lattarumpēr | poṭittaṭaṅ kāmulai pūlantait temmaṉṉar pūvaḻiya | viṭittaṭaṅ kāvuru mēntiya kōṉkolli yīrmpoḻilvāy | vaṭittaṭaṅ kaṇṇiṇai yāleṉṉai vāṭṭiya vāṇutaṟkē ||

Cf. TK29: viḻiyāṟ piṇaiyām viḷaṅkiya lāṉmayi lāmiḻaṟṟum | moḻiyāṟ kiḷiyā mutuvā ṉavarta muṭittokaikaḷ | kaḻiyāk kaḻaṟṟillaik kūttaṉ kayilaimut tammalaittēṉ | koḻiyāt tikaḻum poḻiṟkeḻi lāmeṅ kulateyvamē ||

UVC presumably emended here to fit the metre and quasi-etukai. There is no manuscript evidence for the readings he chose, and aru niṟai, the initial emendation, is not a common collocation in the old corpus. Though this and the other reading — aru nilai — make sense, so does the palm-leaf reading. Without a universally applied metre or 'rhyme-scheme', there isn't good enough reason to emend.

Cf. PK39 (IAC3, p.69): taṇtā talarkaṇṇi yaṇṇaṟtaṉ ṉuḷḷan taḷarvuceyta | vaṇṭār kuḻaliva ḷēyivaḷ māṉīr maṇaṟṟimaṅkai | viṇṭā ruṭaṟkuṉṟa mēṟi viḻikaṭ kaḻukuṟaṅkak | kaṇtāṉ potiyi lituvē yavaṉcoṉṉa kārppuṉamē ||

Cf. PK40 (IAC3, p.69): ciṉamu maḻintu ceruviṭait tōṟṟatev vēntarkaḷpōyk | kaṉavum paṭikaṭai yaṟceṟṟa vēntaṉ karuṅkuḻalār | maṉamum vaṭikkaṇṇun taṅkuman tārattu maṉṉaṉkollip | puṉamu mituviva ḷēyavaṉ ṟāṉkaṇṭa pūṅkoṭiyē||

Cf. TK32: vaṭukka ṇivaivañci yañci miṭaiyitu vāypavaḷam | tuṭikkiṉ ṟavāveṟpaṉ coṟpari cēyāṉ ṟeṭarntuviṭā | viṭiccanta māmala raṇṇalviṇ ṇōrvaṇaṅ kampalampōl | paṭiccanta mumitu vēyiva ḷēyap paṇimoḻiyē ||
Cf. IAC3, p.70: iṟantupaṭātu eṉkārum vanta iruntuṇaip peruntakkāṉai yāṉ periyatōriṭaṉaṉṟik kaḻaṟiṉēṉeṉa niṉaikkum.
Cf. PK42.4 (IAC3, p.70): ariya malarneṭuṅ kaṇkaṇṭu mālaṇṇa lāṟṟiyatē ||
The TNV poem has much more in common with the IAC prose preamble to these poems, though there is some similarity to the line proved from PK42.
Cf. PN21.5: katirnuḻai kallā marampayil kaṭimiḷai |
Cf. Perum374: veyinuḻai paṟiyāk kuyiṉuḻai potumpark |

Cf. IAC3, p.71: iravō raṉṉa koḻuniḻaṟ ṟāay | nilavō raṉṉa veṇmaṇa loḻuki | vaṇṭun tēṉum varikkaṭaip piracamuṅ | koṇṭu puṇarnaram peṉṟu muraṉṟu | viḻaivu viṭutta viḻumi yōrkkum | viḻaivu tōṟṟu nikkum ||
Cf. PK53.4 (IAC3, p.75): ērmaṉṉu kōtaiyaip pōliṉi tāyiṟṟiv vīrmpoḻilē ||
Cf. NA185.11: viṉaimāṇ pāvai yaṉṉōḷ |
Cf. TK38.3-4: kayilaip payilpuṉamum | tēmpiṇai vārkuḻa lāḷeṉat tōṉṟumeṉ cintaṉaikkē ||
Cf. IAC3, p.72: niṉṉai yāṉ ippoḻiluṟai teyvameṉa aiyuṟuval, allaiyāyiṉ vāytiṟavāy. āvi ceṉṟāpiṉṉaip peyarppataritaṉrē yeṉa avvakai

Cf. PK46 (IAC3, p.72): mēviyoṉ ṉāraiveṇ māttuveṉ ṟāṉkaṉṉi vīḻpoḻilvāyt | tēviyeṉ ṟāniṉṉai yāṉiṉaik kiṉṟatu cēyaripāy | kāviveṉ ṟāyakaṇ ṇāyallai yēloṉṟu kaṭṭuraiyā | yāviceṉṟāṟ piṉṉai yāro peyarppa rakaliṭattē ||

Cf. PK47 (IAC3, p.72): tiraiyuṟai vārpuṉaṟ cēvūrc cerumaṉṉar cīraḻitta | vuraiyuṟai tīntamiḻ vēnta ṉucitaṉoṇ pūmpotilyil | varaiyuṟai teyvameṉ ṟēṟkallai yēluṉṟaṉ vāytiṟavāy | viraiyuṟai kōtai uyircelliṉ yārpiṟar mīṭpavarē ||
Cf. TK41.2-4: tēviyeṉ ṟēyaiyañ ceṉṟataṉ ṟēyaṟi yacciṟitum | māviyaṉ ṟaṉṉameṉ ṉōkkiniṉ vāytiṟa vāviṭileṉ | āviyaṉ ṟēyamiḻ tēyaṇaṅ kēyiṉ ṟaḻikiṉṟatē ||

With regards to the geminated p, not present in C1, there is an almost identical syntactic construction with cevvāy in KT334.1, where there is gemination and no discrepancy amongst the witnesses. There is no reason why it should not geminate, so this was most likely a scribal error, although cevvāy is reported as a variant by Pāvaṉantam Piḷḷai, without gemination. My impression is that it is quite a common feature C1, to either present unnecessary or incorrect gemination, or to miss necessary and correct gemination.
